summaries.Volcano and caldera: The January 2022 eruption site was near the uninhabited islands Hunga Tonga and Hunga Ha'apai, which are described as elongated remnants of the ridge around a large submarine caldera atop an active volcano, with the islands representing the visible northern and western remnants of that ridge. Samples from Hunga Tonga revealed alternating layers of andesitic lava flows and beds of scoria, lapilli, and ash dipping gently away from the center of the circle, supporting the hypothesis of a large submarine caldera. High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys confirmed a 4 × 2 kilometer, 150-meter-deep depression in the location of the anticipated submarine caldera, a feature formed by the explosive collapse of a previous volcanic structure during an earlier eruption. The January 2022 eruption was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera, potentially ranking as high as 5 on the volcanic explosivity index (VEI). The hitherto joined islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Ha'apai, covering just over 1 km² with a maximum altitude of 149 m, were blown apart by the eruption, with only very small fragments remaining at the western and eastern edges. The 2014–2015 eruptions had previously created a new volcanic cone joining the two islands, and sonar surveys at that time revealed the large central caldera and features of previous eruptions.
summaries.Geography: Hunga volcano is located along the Tonga segment of the Tonga-Kermadec intra-oceanic arc in the Pacific Ocean, formed as a result of the subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate, within a massive subduction system extending 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga. The volcano rises more than 1.8 km high and extends approximately 20 km across the seafloor, remaining mostly submerged, with its summit featuring a large caldera approximately 5 km in diameter. Only two small ends of this caldera emerge from the water, forming the two small uninhabited islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Ha'apai. The 2014/15 eruption created a new conjoined island, Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'apai, when tephra from the eruption built up around a new volcanic cone connecting the two existing islands. Shockwaves from the January 2022 eruption were felt in Fiji approximately 500 kilometers northwest, New Zealand 1,600–2,000 kilometers away, and Alaska 9,370 kilometers northeast, with pressure waves traveling around the world three times before dissipating. The new island formed in 2014 has been the subject of detailed morphological studies monitoring erosion processes, with research also providing potential insights into similar landform features observed on Mars.
summaries.Eruptions: The Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai submarine volcano (20.54° S, 175.38° W) erupted on 15 January 2022, producing a vertical plume more than 30 km tall with overshooting tops above 55 km, a record in the satellite era, and spewing volcanic material up to 58 km into the atmosphere. The single-event energy release from the initial explosion is estimated at between 10 and 28 EJ, likely larger than the 1991 Mt Pinatubo eruption (around 10 EJ) and possibly comparable to Krakatoa in 1883 (around 30 EJ), making it the largest eruption recorded anywhere on the planet in 30 years and the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern scientific instruments. The eruption caused widespread destruction to Tonga, brought a nearly 15 m tsunami that destroyed villages, and created a sonic boom that rippled around the world twice. It released between 0.5 and 1.5 metric megatons of sulfur dioxide into the stratosphere, producing sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight and reduce surface radiative forcing, though this aerosol load was not very large compared to the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption which released about 20 metric megatons of sulfur dioxide. In the year following the eruption, the cooling effect from volcanic aerosols reflecting sunlight was stronger than the warming caused by water vapor trapping heat, but most effects had dissipated by the end of 2023, with the global decrease in radiative flux remaining less than 0.25 watt per square meter over the 2-year period, and the aerosol effects mostly confined to the Southern Hemisphere in 2022 and 2023.
